coredata.dc:title: Indoor synthesis of carbon quantum dots and its potential applications study as tracers in oilfields
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Petroleum Science and Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Xinjiong
Yan, Liwei
Fan, Siwei
Huang, Yuaojie
Xu, Hongwei
Lai, Nanjun
coredata.dc:description: Feasibility of carbon quantum dots as tracers in petroleum industry was explored in this study to provide diversified options for reservoir evaluation. A kind of water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CA-CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield and an average particle size of ca. 5.6 nm was successfully prepared with citric acid as carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source by controlling hydrothermal temperature and time. Fluorescence test results demonstrated that CA-CQDs were easy to sample and analyse and present outstanding fluorescence performance at 10−6 g L−1. The stability, adsorption, compatibility and fluidity of CA-CQDs were comprehensively evaluated. Results showed that optical properties of CA-CQDs present excellent stability because they minimally changed under different temperatures, salt solutions and pH conditions. Adsorption effect of different minerals on CA-CQDs was small, and concentration retention rate was stable at above 90% after 5 days of adsorption. CA-CQDs showed satisfactory compatibility with formation water samples, and concentration deviation was maintained within 2%. It was convenient for real-time monitoring and analysis because CA-CQDs presented evident breakthrough–elution process with strong migration capacity but weak retention and adsorption capacities when flowing in the pipeline. The results of this study proved that CA-CQDs demonstrated high application potential as tracers in oilfields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tracer
Nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence property
Oilfield application

Adsorption resistance of CA-CQDs based on simulated formation rocks
Compatibility with oilfield injected and produced waters

Synthesis and fluorescence properties of CA-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of CA-CQDs and fluorescence phenomenon.
(a) TEM image of CA-CQDs. (b) FTIR spectra of CA and CA-CQDs. (c) UV–Vis spectra of different CA-CQDs concentrations. (d) Standard absorbance–concentration curve of CA-CQDs.

(a) FL spectra of CA-CQDs with different concentrations. (b) Standard FL intensity–concentration curve of CA-CQDs. (c) Emission spectra of CA-CQDs with different excitation wavelengths. (d) TGA of CA-CQDs. (e) Zeta potential and particle size analysis of CA-CQDs.

(a) The determination of fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs at different temperatures. (b) The determination of absorbance of CA-CQDs at different temperatures. (c) The determination of concentration retention of CA-CQDs at 60 °C. (d) The determination of concentration retention of CA-CQDs at 80 °C.

(a) The determination of fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs in different salt solution systems at 20 °C. (b) The determination of absorbance of CA-CQDs in different salt solution systems at 20 °C. (c) The determination of fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs in different salt solution systems at 85 °C. (d) The determination of absorbance of CA-CQDs in different salt solution systems at 85 °C. (e) The determination of fluorescence intensity and absorbance of CA-CQDs under different pH conditions.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs after static adsorption in rock samples from different strata. (b) Absorbance of CA-CQDs after static adsorption in rock samples from different strata. (c) Different rock samples were statically adsorbed by FL spectrophotometry test the concentration retention rate of CA-CQDs. (d) Different rock samples were statically adsorbed by UV–Vis spectrophotometry test the concentration retention rate of CA-CQDs.

(a) Compatibility and concentration deviation of CA-CQDs with formation injected water. (b) Compatibility and concentration deviation of CA-CQDs with formation produced water. (c) Relationship curve of accumulation and injection volume during the CA-CQD flow test. (d) Relationship curve of relative concentration and injection volume during the CA-CQD flow test.

head.ce:title: Indoor synthesis of carbon quantum dots and its potential applications study as tracers in oilfields

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Oil and Gas Fields Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu City, Sichuan Province, 610500, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Oil and Gas Fields Applied Chemistry Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Feasibility of carbon quantum dots as tracers in petroleum industry was explored in this study to provide diversified options for reservoir evaluation. A kind of water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CA-CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield and an average particle size of ca. 5.6 nm was successfully prepared with citric acid as carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source by controlling hydrothermal temperature and time. Fluorescence test results demonstrated that CA-CQDs were easy to sample and analyse and present outstanding fluorescence performance at 10−6 g L−1. The stability, adsorption, compatibility and fluidity of CA-CQDs were comprehensively evaluated. Results showed that optical properties of CA-CQDs present excellent stability because they minimally changed under different temperatures, salt solutions and pH conditions. Adsorption effect of different minerals on CA-CQDs was small, and concentration retention rate was stable at above 90% after 5 days of adsorption. CA-CQDs showed satisfactory compatibility with formation water samples, and concentration deviation was maintained within 2%. It was convenient for real-time monitoring and analysis because CA-CQDs presented evident breakthrough–elution process with strong migration capacity but weak retention and adsorption capacities when flowing in the pipeline. The results of this study proved that CA-CQDs demonstrated high application potential as tracers in oilfields.

ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, tracers are markers introduced into the system to study the behavior or properties of a specified process. At present, tracers are mainly divided into radioactive tracers and non-radioactive tracers (Pu et al., 2016; Torrealba et al., 2019). A large number of radioactive tracers have been restricted in recent years due to their disadvantages of high cost, complicated operation and toxicity to the environment. Nonradioactive tracers, such as thiocyanate (SCN−), halide ions (Cl−, Br− and I−) and fluorobenzoic acid (FBA) derivatives (Wang et al., 2018), have been commonly used as tracers between wells due to their wide variety, low cost and low toxicity. However, these tracers also present some disadvantages, such as poor stability, easy interaction with minerals and adsorption (Zhao et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2020; Franco et al., 2020; Sharma et al., 2012; Mauricio et al., 2021). Hence, water-soluble fluorescent substances are considered ideal tracers and preferred by oilfield workers because of their satisfactory stability, low dosage, high analytical accuracy, compatibility with injected fluids and low adsorption on formation rocks (AlAbbad et al., 2019; Smith et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2012; Cao et al., 2020).

Carbon quantum dots are a new type of carbon nanomaterial with fluorescent properties first discovered in 2004 (Xu et al. 2004, 2007) and has been widely used in biomedical imaging (Jana et al., 2019; Pichardo-Molina et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2019; Tripathi et al., 2017a,b), photocatalytic reaction (Li et al., 2019; Tripathi et al., 2017a,b; Sai et al., 2018) and water treatment fields (Nasrollahzadeh et al., 2021; Park et al., 2020; Zhao and Chung, 2018). This nanomaterial consists of carbon core and surface group. The carbon core is a skeleton composed of sp2-hybridised graphite microcrystalline carbon and sp3-hybridised amorphous carbon, and its surface often shows abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Hence, carbon quantum dots exhibit excellent stability and water solubility. In addition, a rigid planar structure forms in the carbon core and the energy potential well created on the surface traps additional electrons in it to ensure that it can emit strong fluorescence when excited (Singh et al., 2019; Langer et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2018). Extensive research (Yang et al., 2020; Panwar et al., 2019; Myint et al., 2018; Zhong et al., 2018) in the field of biomedicine showed that carbon quantum dots present strong migration ability and more advantages in terms of luminous intensity and stability compared with traditional organic dyes. This nanomaterial also inherits the biocompatibility of carbon materials and can meet real-time monitoring requirements of complex biological environments. Carbon sources for preparing carbon quantum dots come from a wide range of sources, and carbon quantum dots are less dosage in the tracing process, so they have low use cost. However, studies on the application of carbon quantum dots as tracers in oil fields in the petroleum industry are limited. Size control and fluorescence intensity are key elements in this application. Not only is it required not to be adsorbed and retained during the tracing process, but also facilitate the capture and monitoring of fluorescence signal analysis.

Based on this idea, we designed an N-doped carbon quantum dot. Electronic environment inside and on the surface of the carbon quantum dot changes, and its fluorescence properties are effectively improved with the introduction of the N element. Meanwhile, we selected anhydrous citric acid rich in carboxyl groups as the carbon source, and ethylenediamine, which is rich in amino groups as the nitrogen source, and then synthesised N-doped citrate carbon quantum dots (named CA-CQDs) via hydrothermal method. Intermolecular dehydration and polymerisation of raw materials formed a stable carbon skeleton in the hydrothermal process. Meanwhile, ethylenediamine reacted with abundant functional groups on the surface of the carbon skeleton to form N-doped carbon quantum dots. Small-sized carbon quantum dots present a large specific surface area and a large number of electrons are trapped by the surface energy potential well. Thus, carbon quantum dots can obtain high fluorescence quantum yield. On this basis, we adjusted the size of carbon quantum dots by controlling the hydrothermal temperature and time. As expected, CA-CQDs with an average particle size of 5.36 nm showed excellent fluorescence characteristics. The influence of molar ratio of citric acid to ethylenediamine on the fluorescence performance of CA-CQDs was investigated in detail. Apart from adsorption and compatibility, Zeta potential of CA-CQDs under different pH conditions was measured. The prepared material was also tested for stability under different temperature, salinity and pH conditions. Lastly, migration breakthrough and tracing abilities of CA-CQDs were explored through core flow experiments.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) are all analytical grades, and they are all purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd., Chengdu, China.

The water system filter membrane was purchased from Haining Zhengxing Special Filter Equipment Manufacturing Co., Ltd., Zhejiang, China. Dialysis bags were purchased from Beijing Soleibao Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China. Quartz sand was purchased from Chengdu Zhanghua Purification Technology Co., Ltd., Chengdu, China. The oil sand is prepared by stirring and saturated adsorption of crude oil from Shengli Oilfield (Shandong, China) and 80 mesh quartz sand at 70 °C. Montmorillonite powder was purchased from Tuoyi New Material Co., Ltd., Guangzhou, China. The core was purchased from Chengdu Haohan Completion Rock Power Experiment Center, Chengdu, China. Unless otherwise specified, the water used in this study is ultrapure water.

CA-CQDs with a molar ratio of anhydrous citric acid (CA) to ethylenediamine (EDA) of 1:1 was first prepared. CA (1.06 g, 0.00552 mol) was dissolved in 20 mL of pure water and then 370 μL of EDA (0.00553 mol) was added into the mixture. The mixture was subsequently transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave to react for 6 h at 200 °C. The resulting solution was centrifuged at a speed of 12,000 r min−1 for 20 min and supernatant was extracted and filtered through a water system membrane with a diameter of 0.22 μm after the reaction temperature was cooled to room temperature. The filtrate was then continuously dialysed for 12 h in pure water using a dialysis bag with a molecular retention of 1000. Lastly, brown CA-CQD powder was obtained after the dialysed solution was freeze-dried. Other CA-CQDs with different molar ratios of CA to EDA were also prepared in a similar way for the comparison of fluorescence.

A Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin (FEI Corporation, USA) TEM with an operating voltage of 100 kV was used to observe samples. A carbon-coated copper TEM grid was utilised as the substrate, and CA-CQD droplets were placed on the copper grid and dried. The dried copper grid was then placed on the sample rack for TEM imaging.
The surface structure of CA-CQDs was characterised using a WQF-520 FTIR (Beijing Ruili Analytical Instrument Company) with a scanning range of 400–4400 cm−1 via KBr pellet technique.

UV–Vis spectra were measured using a UV-2600 (Shimadzu Enterprise Management China Co., Ltd.) UV–Vis spectrophotometer with a scanning range of 200–700 nm. UV–Vis spectra of CA-CQD solutions with concentrations of 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04 and 0.05 g L−1 were tested. The absorbance–concentration standard curve was fitted to facilitate subsequent experiments and determine the concentration of CA-CQDs through absorbance. The absorbance test of the CA-CQD solution involved in this article used the same solution that removed CA-CQDs as the reference solution. For example, the MgCl2 solution of the same concentration was used as the reference solution to measure the absorbance of CA-CQDs in the MgCl2 solution.

A Perkin–Elmer LS55 (Perkin–Elmer Instruments, LLC) FL spectrophotometer was used to test the fluorescence performance. The excitation spectrum range was from 260 nm to 400 nm, and the emission spectrum range was from 360 nm to 600 nm. FL spectra of CA-CQD solutions were tested with concentrations of 2 × 10−5, 4 × 10−5, 6 × 10−5, 8 × 10−5 and 10 × 10−5 g L−1. We also fitted the standard curve because the detection limit of the fluorescence spectrum was several orders of magnitude lower than that of the ultraviolet spectrum.
TGA was carried out with a Labsys EVO (France Seitaram) synchronous thermal analyser at a temperature range of 30 °C–800 °C and a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in air atmosphere.

The Zeta potential and particle size of CA-CQDs were measured with a Zeta PALS 190 Plus (manufacturer, Brookhaven, USA) Zeta potential and particle size analyser under test conditions of pH of 2–12 and fixed solution concentration of 1 × 10−3 g L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effectiveness of CA-CQDs solution based on different temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: CA-CQDs solution of 2 × 10−4 g L−1 was prepared with deionised water, and the fluorescence intensity of CA-CQDs was measured at 24 °C (room temperature), 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C, 70 °C, 80 °C and 90 °C to investigate the temperature resistance of CA-CQDs. In addition, 0.02 g L−1 of the CA-CQDs solution was prepared and the absorbance was measured at the same temperature values to explore the temperature resistance of CA-CQDs in the concentration range of different orders of magnitude. We carried out several experiments in 30 days, and converted the test results into concentration by fitting the standard curve, and illustrated the concentration retention curve of CA-CQDs within 30 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Resistance of CA-CQDs based on different metal cations and concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: KCl, NaCl and MgCl2 (10 g L−1) solutions were prepared with deionised water. The CA-CQDs solution was mixed with each salt solution system according to a volume ratio of 1:1 and then placed on a magnetic stirrer to disperse evenly for 30 min. The fluorescence intensity and absorbance were measured to assess the salt resistance of CA-CQDs after stirring and dispersing evenly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of CA-CQDs at different pH values
ce:section.ce:para: We needed to determine the stability of CA-CQDs under strong acid and strong base conditions, so the pH range was set from 2 to 12 in this test. We prepared 1 g L−1 of the CA-CQDs solution in a volumetric flask and respectively diluted CA-CQDs to concentrations convenient for fluorescence intensity and absorbance measurements in separate volumetric flasks by pipette. Then, we divided the CA-CQDs solution into 11 groups, and adjusted the pH of each group of CA-CQDs solution with HCl and NaOH (1 mol L−1) standard solutions. Lastly, we measured the fluorescence intensity and absorbance after stirring to evaluate the pH stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption resistance of CA-CQDs based on simulated formation rocks
ce:section.ce:para: Evident adsorption of many tracers by formation rocks and their intense embedding in pores in the actual formation tracing process affected the accuracy of the tracing results and remarkably reduced their tracing ability (Façanha et al., 2020; Li et al. 2016). Therefore, we used quartz sand, oil sand, montmorillonite and artificial core fragments to simulate formation rocks and then carried out adsorption experiments of CA-CQDs to evaluate adsorption resistance and migration ability of CA-CQDs. We placed 40 mL of the CA-CQDs solution and 10 g of each solid rock sample into 50 mL plastic centrifuge tubes and then used a magnetic stir bar for stirring. We utilised FL and UV–Vis spectrophotometers to carry out a 5-day tracking test and analysed the concentration retention rate of CA-CQDs within 5 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compatibility with oilfield injected and produced waters
ce:section.ce:para: Tracers were often injected together with other fluids during the injection process, and flocculation and precipitation that may occur between tracers and other fluids due to some influences could reduce the tracer effect. Therefore, we collected injected and produced waters from the Shengtuo area of Shengli Oilfield and then filtered them through a 0.22 μm water system filter membrane. CA-CQDs were mixed with injected and produced water samples in a volume ratio of 1:1 and 1:3 and then placed at the oil layer temperature of 60 °C and 80 °C, respectively, for observation. We measured the concentration of CA-CQDs several times within 5 days and analysed the concentration deviation to examine the compatibility of CA-CQDs comprehensively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CA-CQDs injection test based on the physical model of sand-filled pipe
ce:section.ce:para: Core flow experiment (Chang et al., 2019; Cox et al., 2017; Yao et al., 2017). We used 60–80 mesh quartz sand to fill a 30-cm sand-filled pipe, and used this to replace the core for flow experiment. Two sand-filled pipes with similar permeability were injected respectively with 4.5 PV CA-CQDs and NaCl solutions diluted to 0.1 g L−1 with injected water after measuring the permeability of water flooding. Lastly, the post-water flooding stage was carried out. Meanwhile, we collected the produced fluids at different stages in the process, and measured the fluorescence intensity and conductivity to obtain the concentration of CA-CQDs and NaCl respectively. After that, we drew breakthrough elution curves of CA-CQDs and NaCl to compare the breakthrough migration capabilities of CA-CQDs and NaCl. In addition, we filled sand-filled pipes with different permeability between 100 and 2000 × 10−3 μm2, and carried out the same experimental process as above. After injecting CA-CQDs into sand-filled pipes with different permeability, the permeability of the system after the pressure in the post-water flooding stage was stable was measured. Damage of CA-CQDs to the reservoir based on changes in permeability was evaluated (see Tables 1–3 and 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and fluorescence properties of CA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The filtrate of the mixture solution was placed at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm after reacting for 6 h. The presence of a certain amount of blue fluorescence intensity indicated that CA-CQDs with fluorescence properties were successfully prepared. Compared with other carbon quantum dots (Jeong et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2021; Roshni et al., 2021; Christopoulou et al., 2021), CA-CQDs demonstrated higher fluorescence quantum yield and intensity. Citric acid and ethylenediamine could fully react to form a conjugated system and a rigid planar structure conducive to the generation of fluorescence because of their abundant carboxyl and amino groups (He et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2020). In addition, the molar ratio of CA to EDA exerted a significant effect on fluorescence properties of synthesised CA-CQDs. As shown in Table 4
, the higher fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of the prepared CA-CQDs were obtained as a result of the higher molar ratio of EDA to CA. This might be because the resultant CA-CQDs with higher ratio of EDA had more N groups on the surface, which provided more energy traps trapping electrons, for emitting stronger fluorescence when CA-CQDs were excited. Fluorescence properties of CA-CQDs began to decline when the molar ratio of EDA to CA further increased because the excess EDA hindered the interaction between CA and EDA, including fracturing and reconstruction of intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds, and deteriorated fluorescence properties of CA-CQDs.

Morphological features of CA-CQDs were observed via TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The HRTEM image showed that CA-CQDs were near-spherical particles with uniform shape and size and an average particle size of approximately 5.36 nm. FT-IR spectra of CA and CA-CQDs were presented in Fig. 2b. Several characteristic peaks were observed at 3359 (stretching vibration of O–H), 2914 (stretching vibration of C–H) and 1725 (stretching vibration of CO) cm−1. CA-CQDs demonstrated multiple absorption bands from 3500 cm−1 to 3800 cm−1 likely caused by O–H tensile vibration in different environments. The weak absorption band at 3233 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of N–H in the amide group verified that CA-CQDs successfully introduced the N element through dehydration of carboxyl and amino groups. Double peaks at 1694 and 1649 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibration of CO in the amide group. Continuous absorption bands at 1511 cm−1 corresponded to the skeletal vibration of the benzene ring and the peak at 1057 cm−1 was due to the bending vibration of C–H in the aromatic ring. This finding indicated that CA-CQDs might show acceptable stability because the surface of CA-CQDs contained some aromatic ring structures (see Fig. 1).

UV–Vis spectra of CA-CQDs were illustrated in Fig. 2c. CA-CQDs demonstrated a strong absorption band in the near-ultraviolet light region due to the typical absorption of π–π* transition of CC (Wu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2016). The characteristic absorption peak at 340 nm was caused by n–π* transition of CO in CA-CQDs (Ding et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2021). The increase of absorbance of CA-CQDs with the increase of concentration exhibited a satisfactory linear relationship with a maximum linear correlation coefficient of 0.9962 (Fig. 2d). Therefore, absorbance could be used for quantitative analysis of CA-CQDs concentration.

As shown in fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3 a, CA-CQDs exhibited a strong fluorescence absorption band in the range of 320–360 nm and a strong fluorescence emission band in the range of 415–455 nm. Optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CA-CQDs were 340 and 435 nm, respectively. The satisfactory mirror-image relationship of excitation and emission spectra indicated that molecules minimally changed when transiting from the ground state to the excited state and presented the same interval of vibration level. In addition, the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of CA-CQDs exhibited an acceptable linear relationship with a maximum linear correlation coefficient of 0.9971 (Fig. 3b). Notably, CA-CQDs exhibited excitation wavelength dependence. The fluorescence intensity of emission spectra first increased and then decreased when the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 300 nm to 400 nm. The fluorescence intensity reached the maximum at 340 nm and the corresponding emission peak red shifted as the excitation wavelength increased (Fig. 3c). Fluorescence tunability of as-prepared CA-CQDs was presumably due to inhomogeneity of particle sizes and abundant emissive traps of surface states formed by hydroxyl, carboxyl and aminol functional groups (Tang et al., 2021; Ding et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2021). This characteristic could be applied to fluorescence resonance energy transfer technology.

The thermal stability of CA-CQDs was investigated using TGA (Fig. 3d). Mass of the CA-CQDs sample decreased gradually with the increase of temperature. The sample demonstrated partial weight loss at a temperature below 150 °C due to the gradual volatilisation of water molecules wrapped by CA-CQDs material during the heating process. The sample began to lose weight uniformly when the temperature increased to 200 °C because oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CA-CQDs decomposed into CO2 and H2O. CC on the aromatic ring in CA-CQDs began to fracture when the temperature reached 400 °C. The amide group also began to crack and might be accompanied by the combustion decomposition of a portion of the carbon skeleton with the continuous increase of temperature. Hence, the rate of weightlessness was slightly accelerated. The TGA results indicated that CA-CQDs could exist stably under a reservoir temperature of 200 °C.

As the pH gradually increased from acidic to alkaline, the particle size of CA-CQDs first became smaller and then larger, and the Zeta potential of the dispersion also showed a similar change, from −37.88 mV to −38.85 mV then to −37.04 mV, which was shown in Fig. 3e. When the pH was weakly acidic, the particle size of CA-CQDs reached a minimum of 6.45 nm, and the Zeta potential also reached a minimum of −38.85 mV. It showed that the particle size could affect the Zeta potential of CA-CQDs. In general, the absolute value of the Zeta potential of CA-CQDs under different pH conditions was always higher than 30 mV, indicating that the CA-CQDs dispersion was very stable, had good dispersion, and was less affected by pH.

Fig. 4 a and b displayed the effect of temperature on optical properties of CA-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity and absorbance of CA-CQDs decreased slightly as the temperature increased after 30 days of testing and analysis. The increase in temperature intensified the thermal motion of molecules, increased the probability of collisions, enhanced the external conversion energy and decreased the optical performance. We focused on the stability of CA-CQDs at 60 °C and 80 °C. The concentration retention curve of CA-CQDs was obtained according to the linear relationship between optical properties of CA-CQDs and the concentration (Fig. 4c and d). The concentration of CA-CQDs changed smoothly and the concentration retention rate stabilised at above 90% within 30 days. This finding indicated the excellent temperature resistance of CA-CQDs.

CA-CQDs also presented excellent salt resistance. The change in fluorescence intensity and absorbance of CA-CQDs was negligible after adding CA-CQDs to 10 g L−1 of KCl, NaCl and MgCl2 and their mixed salt solution system, as shown in Fig. 5 a and b. In addition, when the above mixed solution systems were placed at 85 °C, fluorescence and absorbance test results of CA-CQDs were similar to those at 20 °C, as shown in Fig. 5c and d. This finding indicated that satisfactory chemical stability and optical properties of CA-CQDs remained unaffected by the high salinity salt solution. Therefore, CA-CQDs could be used as tracers in high-salinity oil reservoirs.

The fluorescence intensity and absorbance change curves of CA-CQDs under different pH conditions were shown in Fig. 5e. The closer the pH was next to neutral, the stronger the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of CA-CQDs. As acidity and alkalinity increased, the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of CA-CQDs decreased. In addition, it was also found that the optical properties of CA-CQDs were more affected by alkaline conditions. Therefore, CA-CQDs were more suitable for weak acid and weak alkaline reservoirs. In general, CA-CQDs had a small decrease in fluorescence intensity and absorbance, and had good pH stability.

The adsorption experiment results showed that adsorption degree of CA-CQDs on quartz sand, oil sand, montmorillonite and artificial core fragments was very low. The decrease of fluorescence intensity and absorbance of CA-CQDs was insignificant after 5 days of stirring and adsorption (Fig. 6 a and b). The retention rate of CA-CQDs concentration flattened and stabilised at above 90% as the adsorption time increased (Fig. 6c and d). This finding showed that the simulation formation rock samples exerted a small adsorption effect on CA-CQDs. Meanwhile, CA-CQDs still had a high concentration retention rate in oil sand, which indicated that their interaction with oil was very limited. Hence, CA-CQDs presented strong migration ability and failed to accumulate in pores. Furthermore, low adsorption of CA-CQDs on formation rock samples met the requirements of field application.

CA-CQDs exhibited acceptable compatibility with formation injected and produced waters. The mixed solution constantly remained clear without suspension, flocculation and sedimentation when CA-CQDs were mixed with injected and produced waters in different volume ratios and maintained for several days. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the concentration of CA-CQDs decreased slightly as the compatibility time of CA-CQDs with injected and produced waters increased. We compared each measurement result with the initial concentration and obtained the curve of concentration deviation over time. The change of the concentration deviation was gradual and the concentration deviation was stable within 2% with the increase of time. The results showed that CA-CQDs presented satisfactory compatibility and met the requirements of cross-well tracing on formation compatibility.

Water-soluble fluorescent CA-CQDs were successfully prepared via hydrothermal reaction with CA as the carbon source and EDA as the nitrogen source in this study. The CA-CQDs material with an average particle size of 5.36 nm presented strong migration ability in pores due to its high solubility in the water phase. CA-CQDs also exhibited excellent optical properties and stability, low dosage and long service life. This material met screening conditions of oilfield water injection tracers after a series of oilfield tracer applicability evaluations. Moreover, carbon sources for preparing CQDs were widely available and the use cost was very low. Therefore, the development of different types of CQDs as a new type of oilfield tracer according to geological conditions of oilfields presented high application potential. The results of this study could provide guidance for the preparation of CQDs materials in an in-depth and comprehensive manner, solving practical problems and broadening application fields of CQDs.

Xinjiong Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Experiments conducting, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Liwei Yan: Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Siwei Fan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Experiments conducting. Yuaojie Huang: Investigation, Experiments conducting. Hongwei Xu: Investigation, Experiments conducting. Nanjun Lai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing–review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Sichuan Provincial Key Laboratory of Applied Chemistry for Oil and Gas Field Open Fund (YQKF202010), Southwest Petroleum University Enhanced Oil Recovery and Oilfield Agents Research Group (2019CXTD03), Sichuan Provincial Department of Science and Technology Project (18YYJC1664), Southwest Petroleum University students Extracurricular Open Experiment Project (2020KSZ33001, 2020KSZ04047).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
